questions.question: Explain the difference between a singly linked list and a doubly linked list. Also state one advantage and one disadvantage of linked lists over arrays.
keyPoints: Singly linked list has one pointer (next) per node, doubly linked list has two (prev and next)
Singly allows only forward traversal, doubly allows both forward and backward
Advantage over arrays: Dynamic size, efficient insertion/deletion
Disadvantage: No random access, extra memory for pointers

questions.question: Write an algorithm to insert a node at the beginning of a singly linked list. Also mention the time complexity of this operation.
keyPoints: Create a new node with given data
Set new node's next pointer to current head
Update head to point to new node
Time complexity: O(1) constant time

questions.question: What is a circular linked list? Differentiate between singly circular and doubly circular linked lists.
keyPoints: Circular linked list: last node's next points to head instead of NULL
Singly circular: only next pointer, creates a single loop
Doubly circular: prev of head points to last node, complete circular connection
No NULL pointer in circular variants

questions.question: Describe the structure of a node in a singly linked list. How is memory allocated for a node in C?
keyPoints: Node has two fields: data and next pointer
Data field stores the actual element
Next pointer stores address of next node or NULL
In C: use malloc() or sizeof() to allocate memory dynamically
Node typically defined using struct with self-referential pointer

questions.question: Write an algorithm to delete a node from the beginning of a singly linked list. What is the time complexity?
keyPoints: Check if list is empty (head == NULL)
Store head->next in a temporary pointer
Free memory of current head
Update head to point to temporary
Time complexity: O(1)

questions.question: Write a complete C program to create a singly linked list with 5 nodes, display all elements, and insert a new node at the beginning and at the end of the list.
keyPoints: Define structure for node with data and next pointer
Create function to insert at beginning
Create function to insert at end (traverse to last node)
Create function to display list (traverse and print)
Main function to create initial list, demonstrate insertions
Handle edge cases: empty list, memory allocation
Free allocated memory to prevent leaks

questions.question: Explain the concept of a header node in linked lists. Write an algorithm to insert a node after a given node in a singly linked list with a header node.
keyPoints: Header node is dummy node at beginning containing no data
Simplifies insertion/deletion at first position
Algorithm: Create new node, set new->next = current->next, set current->next = new
No special case handling needed for first node insertion
Time complexity: O(1) for insertion after a given node

questions.question: Compare arrays and linked lists as data structures. Under what circumstances would you prefer using a linked list over an array? Provide appropriate justifications.
keyPoints: Arrays: Contiguous memory, fixed size, O(1) random access, O(n) insertion/deletion
Linked lists: Scattered memory, dynamic size, O(n) random access, O(1) insertion at head
Prefer linked list when: Size unknown, frequent insertions/deletions, memory is fragmented
Prefer array when: Random access needed, size known, memory is contiguous preferred
Trade-off: Linked lists use extra memory for pointers

questions.question: Write a Python program to implement a doubly linked list with methods for inserting at beginning, inserting at end, deleting from beginning, and displaying the list.
keyPoints: Define Node class with data, prev, and next attributes
Define DoublyLinkedList class with head and tail
Insert at beginning: connect new node between None and current head
Insert at end: connect new node between current tail and None
Delete at beginning: move head to head.next, handle prev pointer
Display: traverse from head using next pointers
Handle edge cases for empty list operations
